text: Hello everyone, I present to you a robotic transabdominal single dock retrorectus repair with upper tar for a post-sternotomy subxiphoid six by four centimeter incisional hernia. This patient developed a subxiphoid incisional hernia from a sternotomy for mitral valve replacement. The hernia contains a transverse colon that is incarcerated and is not reducible. The patient has mild symptoms but has never had any bowel obstruction. In approaching this case, I have a few concerns and questions. First and foremost is, will I be able to close the defect? And will a bilateral upper tar help me close the defect? Or a vertical posterior rectus sheath incision, which is well described, which will release the tunnel that the rectus muscle is in and help me medialize the linea alba and close the defect vertically. In a similar fashion, a horizontal posterior rectus sheath incision potentially could release the rectus muscle and allow it to stretch vertically and allow me to close the defect vertically. The second consideration is will I be able to get good mesh overlap under the diaphragm with a preperitoneal dissection.

Hello everyone, I present to you a robotic transabdominal single dock retrorectus repair with upper tar for a post-sternotomy subxiphoid six by four centimeter incisional hernia. This patient developed a subxiphoid incisional hernia from a sternotomy for mitral valve replacement. The hernia contains a transverse colon that is incarcerated and is not reducible. The patient has mild symptoms but has never had any bowel obstruction. In approaching this case, I have a few concerns and questions. First and foremost is, will I be able to close the defect? And will a bilateral upper tar help me close the defect? Or a vertical posterior rectus sheath incision, which is well described, which will release the tunnel that the rectus muscle is in and help me medialize the linea alba and close the defect vertically. In a similar fashion, a horizontal posterior rectus sheath incision potentially could release the rectus muscle and allow it to stretch vertically and allow me to close the defect vertically. The second consideration is will I be able to get good mesh overlap under the diaphragm with a preperitoneal dissection. Usually I can get three to four centimeters but in this case I wanted more overlap due to the size of the defect and was aiming for over five centimeters and with a preperitoneal dissection under the diaphragm and this lady with who is not very tall I wasn't sure if I could get adequate overlap cephalad.

Usually I can get three to four centimeters but in this case I wanted more overlap due to the size of the defect and was aiming for over five centimeters and with a preperitoneal dissection under the diaphragm and this lady with who is not very tall I wasn't sure if I could get adequate overlap cephalad. Here are some of the robotic technique options that are available to me. ETEP Reef Stopa with upper tar. As many of you know, I love the ETEP operation and think it's great for most incisional hernias. However, in this lady, I didn't feel that it was the best option for several reasons. One being that she has a very loose abdomen and I was worried that the peritoneum was going to be very thin and difficult to develop or maintain, which could potentially leave me with a very large gap between the posterior rectus sheath in addition to the gap at the hernia sac. Also, a lateral doc probably wouldn't allow me to get adequate coverage of the mesh on the ipsilateral side because the tar would not be able to reach as lateral as I would like. Therefore, ETEP was not my first option. The next option was a robotic transabdominal prepartineal with or without posterior rectus sheath release, as I had mentioned earlier, either the vertical or the horizontal release or both. And I felt like this was probably the best option in this lady. I can go in laparoscopically and assess the defect, assess her peritoneal integrity and strength, and also have the option of going retrorectus if necessary if the preperitoneal dissection did not go well. The third option was iPalm plus or minus defect. I used to perform this operation laparoscopically with trans-fascial sutures and tacks along the costum margin but I no longer perform this operation since I don't like the number of trans-fascials needed and the subsequent pain caused by the trans-fascials and also So, you know, the transabdominal preperitoneal and the transabdominal retrorectus repairs I think are far superior because they allow me to overlap the defect well and also I do not need as much fixation of the mesh.

So, you know, the transabdominal preperitoneal and the transabdominal retrorectus repairs I think are far superior because they allow me to overlap the defect well and also I do not need as much fixation of the mesh. The fourth option is what I term TASDR and you'll have to excuse me if this has been described before. TASDR stands for Transabdominal Single Dock Retrorectus Repair. This is different from the usual double dock Rebstopa repair. In this case, I would place three ports in the lower abdomen and then make a horizontal incision in both posterior rectus sheets to enter the retro-rectus space and use a peritoneum in the midline to maintain a single posterior flap and dissect up towards the costal margin. To get under the diaphragm I would perform a bilateral upper tar and this tar can be extended vertically in the lateral portion near the linea semilunaris if I needed to get a wider flap for a wider mesh overlap. This is a good option to start with or I could start with a transabdominal preperitoneal and then convert to a TASDR if necessary if the peritoneum is not very thick or I start putting holes in the peritoneum or I need some kind of component release and this would be a great way to perform a vertical posterior rectus sheath incision plus a horizontal posterior rectus sheath incision. Finally there is the full bilateral tar which I thought would be overkill in this situation. I have four other good options and this would be the last option in my armamentarium.

The surgeon begins the procedure laparoscopically, revealing a large defect in the upper abdomen. The peritoneum appears very thin with minimal preperitoneal fat. Robotic ports are placed, and a force bipolar is used in the left hand.

The surgeon begins dissecting the right retro-rectus space, aiming to reach the costal margin. The caudal edge of the hernia sac is visible, with the surgeon carefully maintaining the integrity of the sac tissue while dissecting close to the fascia. The patient's skin over the sac appears thin and purplish, indicating a delicate dermis. The surgeon successfully identifies an areolar plane outside the hernia sac, facilitating the dissection without needing to cauterize near the dermis.

The surgeon begins dissecting the right retro-rectus space, aiming to reach the costal margin. The dissection involves maintaining the integrity of the fascia and hernia sac tissue, which is visible with a purplish, translucent appearance due to the thin dermis. The surgeon identifies and follows an areolar plane outside the hernia sac, facilitating the sac's removal without cauterizing near the dermis. The dissection progresses to the cephalad edge of the hernia sac, preserving its attachments to the peritoneum under the xiphoid and diaphragm, while identifying and dividing connective tissue mistaken for the sac edge.

The surgeon is at the cephalad edge of the hernia sac, maintaining its attachments to the peritoneum under the xiphoid and diaphragm. They identify and divide connective tissue, using cautery to maintain hemostasis. The linea alba is divided up to the xiphoid, and dissection continues underneath the diaphragm. The surgeon performs an upper TAR, staying close to the diaphragm fibers to avoid creating a hernia. They transition into the space behind the transverse abdominus, dividing connective tissue and exposing the diaphragm and transverse abdominus muscle.

The surgeon uses cautery to maintain hemostasis while dissecting the transition area where the hernia sac meets the peritoneum beneath the xiphoid and diaphragm. The linea alba is divided up to the xiphoid level, and dissection begins underneath the diaphragm and xiphoid. The surgeon identifies the right hemidiaphragm fibers, staying close to them to avoid creating a larger hernia. Dissection continues along a fatty plane near the diaphragm, avoiding the central tendon and pericardium.

The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR), cutting the diaphragm's contribution to the posterior rectus sheath. A small hole is made in the peritoneum, but the transverse salus fascia helps maintain robustness. The dissection transitions from the subdiaphragmatic space to the space between the transverse salus fascia and the transverse abdominus muscle, dividing connective tissue between these spaces. The diaphragm and transverse abdominus muscle are exposed, with the transverse salus fascia brought down. The surgeon dissects the left side, staying close to the diaphragm to avoid entering the Morgagni space, and carefully separates preperitoneal fat from the central tendon for mesh overlap.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection on the patient's left side, staying close to the diaphragm muscle to avoid entering the Morgagni space. The dissection reveals preperitoneal fat easily separating from the central tendon, exposing the heart beating through it. The surgeon then incises the posterior lumen of the internal oblique and transverse abdominis, transecting the upper posterior rectus sheath and removing sternotomy wires. Meticulous hemostasis is maintained to ensure clear visibility of tissue planes. The surgeon divides the diaphragm's contribution to the posterior rectus sheath and separates the transverse abdominus muscle from the transverse salus fascia, creating space for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs an upper abdominal TAR, staying beneath the diaphragm and making an incision in the posterior lumen of the internal oblique and transverse abdominis to transect the upper posterior rectus sheath. Two sternotomy wires are visible and will be removed later. Scar tissue is present, but the diaphragm muscle serves as a guide. The surgeon meticulously maintains hemostasis while dividing the posterior lumbar and anterior oblique, then scoops the transverse abdominus muscle to complete the division. The white layer of the diaphragm contribution to the posterior rectus sheath is cut, and the surgeon enters the space between the transverse abdominus muscle and the transverse salus fascia, dividing intervening fascial tissue to combine spaces under the diaphragm and transverse abdominis. Adequate space is created for the upper lateral part of the mesh, with good cephalad dissection for overlap. The defect is closed vertically using an OV-Lock suture, progressively tensioning to elongate the muscle and relax the abdominal wall, ensuring the defect closes without tearing tissue or breaking sutures.

The surgeon uses an OV-Lock suture to progressively close a wide subxiphoid defect vertically, carefully tensioning the suture to avoid tearing the thin tissue. The closure is facilitated by reducing pneumoperitoneum to 8 mmHg. The surgeon ensures the suture captures both anterior and posterior fascia layers for secure closure. Two sternotomy wires are visible and will be removed later. The surgeon measures the defect and prepares a 15x15 cm piece of mesh for placement, ensuring at least 7 cm of overlap superiorly and inferiorly.

The surgeon cinches the suture by pulling it posteriorly to avoid sawing through the tissue, using a force bipolar to brace the fascia. The mesh is measured, showing a good overlap of at least seven centimeters superior and inferior to the defect. A 15x15 cm piece of medium-weight macroporous polypropylene mesh is prepared, with a purple line drawn for orientation. The surgeon places two interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures down the center to secure the mesh, ensuring it does not shift.

The surgeon secures the medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh in the large space created, using a few 3-0 Vicryl sutures in the center for additional stability. A blue or purple line is drawn down the center of the mesh for orientation. The TASDR procedure, involving horizontal and vertical incisions in the posterior rectus sheath, provides effective component release, allowing closure of the defect with minimal tension.
